Title: A Complete Guide to LLVM for Programming Language Creators
URL: https://mukulrathi.com/create-your-own-programming-language/llvm-ir-cpp-api-tutorial
Content: # A Complete Guide to LLVM for Programming Language Creators. This series of compiler tutorials is for people who don’t just want to create a *toy* language. If you’ve just joined the series at this stage, here’s a quick recap. We’ve desugared our language to get it ready for LLVM - the main takeaway is that objects have been desugared to structs, and their methods desugared to functions. The code for this post (the LLVM IR generation) can be found in the llvm\_ir\_codegen folder. To cut through the boilerplate, to find out how to generate LLVM IR for a particular language expression, search for the `IRCodegenVisitor::codegen` method that takes in the corresponding `ExprIR` object. // this is the LLVM IR generation. So instead of a fixed number of physical registers, in LLVM IR we have an unlimited set of *virtual* registers (labelled `%0`, `%1`, `%2`, `%3`… we can write and read from.

Title: My First Language Frontend with LLVM Tutorial — LLVM 23.0.0git documentation
URL: https://llvm.org/docs/tutorial/MyFirstLanguageFrontend/index.html
Content: # My First Language Frontend with LLVM Tutorial¶. Welcome to the “My First Language Frontend with LLVM” tutorial. This tutorial will get you up and running fast and show a concrete example of something that uses LLVM to generate code. This lets us cover a range of language design and LLVM-specific ideas, showing and explaining the code for it all along the way, and reduces the overwhelming amount of details up front. A lexer is also the first part of building a parser for a language, and we use a simple C++ lexer which is easy to understand. * Chapter #3: Code generation to LLVM IR - with the AST ready, we show how easy it is to generate LLVM IR, and show a simple way to incorporate LLVM into your project. * Chapter #5: Extending the Language: Control Flow - With the basic language up and running, we show how to extend it with control flow operations (‘if’ statement and a ‘for’ loop).

Title: A Gentle Introduction to LLVM IR - mcyoung
URL: https://mcyoung.xyz/2023/08/01/llvm-ir
Content: Now, *I’m* a compiler engineer, so my answer is *of course* you should know your optimizer’s IR. I wrote an introduction to assembly over a year ago (still have to finish the followups… 💀), which I recommend reading first. Clang follows the orthodox compiler architecture: a frontend that parses source code into an AST and lowers it into an *intermediate representation*, an “IR”; an optimizer (or “middle-end”) that transforms IR into better IR, and a backend that converts IR into machine code for a particular platform. Clang, and other language frontends like Rust, essentially compile to LLVM IR, which LLVM then compiles to machine code. LLVM IR is well documented and… *somewhat* stable, which makes it a very good compilation target, since language implementers can re-use the thousands of engineer hours poured into LLVM already. The source of truth for “what is LLVM IR?” is the LangRef. LLVM IR is also binary format (sometimes called “bitcode”), although we will be working exclusively with its text format (which uses the `.ll` extension).
